data.title: XtalPi and Pfizer Expand Strategic Collaboration to Advance AI-Driven Drug Discovery and Materials Science Simulations
data.description:
XtalPi and Pfizer Expand Strategic Collaboration to Advance AI-Driven Drug Discovery and Materials Science Simulations

XtalPi and Pfizer Expand Strategic Collaboration to Advance AI-Driven Drug Discovery and Materials Science Simulations

PR Newswire

CAMBRIDGE, Mass., June 29, 2025

CAMBRIDGE, Mass., June 29, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- XtalPi (2228.HK), a leading global technology company in integrating artificial intelligence (AI) and robotics to advance the discovery of groundbreaking medicine and innovative materials, announced an expansion of its research collaboration with Pfizer to pioneer a next-generation molecular modeling platform for drug discovery. This initiative is focused on improving the accuracy of advanced physics-based methods with the speed, scalability, and expansive coverage of cutting-edge AI models to optimize the discovery and development of small molecule medicines. Building on the success of their existing strategic collaboration, Pfizer and XtalPi will now collaborate on the development of an enhanced platform to grasp Pfizer's rapidly expanding small molecule chemical space, with the goal of delivering accurate predictive tools with significantly improved throughput.

In a joint research paper published in 2024, XtalPi and Pfizer unveiled the development and validation of the first-generation of XtalPi Force Field (XFF), which demonstrated superior performance in predicting small molecules geometry at molecular quantum mechanics (QM) level, as well as accurate Free Energy Perturbation (FEP) calculations for binding affinity predictions. These capabilities are critical for computing and predicting small molecules' key properties such as efficacy and selectivity, enabling more precise drug screening and rational design.

Under this expansion of their collaboration, the two teams will focus on developing more accurate predictive models to Pfizer's proprietary chemical space, with the aim of further empowering small molecule drug discovery and development across a broader range of research applications. In the collaboration, XtalPi will deploy its XFEP platform, from parameter customization to FEP calculations, for Pfizer to use in its drug discovery efforts. With the goals of improved accuracy, high-throughput speed, and user-friendly interface, the platform will be designed to support Pfizer scientists across diverse drug design and development scenarios.

"Pfizer's leadership in pharmaceutical innovation and their deep scientific expertise have been invaluable in shaping the evolution of our AI-driven platform," said Dr. Jian Ma, CEO of XtalPi. "This collaboration demonstrates the transformative potential of XtalPi's computational platform combining physics-based insights with advanced AI technology, and propels us to develop even more powerful predictive tools. Together, we are building up a new platform for drug discovery, potentially unlocking possibilities that were once out of reach and accelerating the delivery of life-changing therapies for patients worldwide."

About XtalPi 

XtalPi Holdings Limited (XtalPi, 2228.HK) was founded in 2015 by three physicists from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT). It is an innovative R&D platform powered by quantum physics, artificial intelligence, and robotics. By integrating first-principles calculations, AI algorithms, high-performance cloud computing, and standardized automation systems, XtalPi provides digital and intelligent R&D solutions for companies in the pharmaceutical, materials science, agricultural technology, energy, new chemicals, and cosmetics industries. 

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/xtalpi-and-pfizer-expand-strategic-collaboration-to-advance-ai-driven-drug-discovery-and-materials-science-simulations-302494033.html

SOURCE XtalPi Inc.

data.title: Caseware Launches Cloud Audit – A Next-Generation Solution for Singapore’s Audit Professionals
data.description:
Singapore, June 29, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Caseware, a global leader in cloud-enabled audit, financial reporting and data analytics solutions, today announced the launch of Cloud Audit, a powerful, cloud-based audit platform purpose-built for Singapore’s audit professionals. 

Designed to accelerate audit transformation, Cloud Audit is aligned with the Institute of Singapore Chartered Accountants (ISCA) methodology, helping firms to meet Singapore’s regulatory and professional standards with confidence. “With the launch of Cloud Audit, we’re bringing a modern, collaborative and secure audit solution tailored to the needs of Singapore’s practitioners,” said Sarah Butler, head of solutions, APAC at Caseware. “Already available and gaining significant traction in Australia, this platform is designed to help firms embrace digital transformation, improve efficiency and stay ahead of evolving compliance requirements.” 

Early adopters of Cloud Audit in other countries across Asia Pacific are already seeing measurable benefits. Accru Felsers, an award-winning accounting firm in Australia, reports improved team collaboration and faster turnaround times since implementing the solution. Jean Zhang, audit and assurance partner, remarked, “With Cloud Audit, we update one template, and every new file reflects that - saving us weeks of work. It's fast, consistent, and accessible from anywhere. After trying it, we wondered why we didn’t make the switch sooner."  

Didarul Khan, director at Auditeo, the next-gen auditors and forensic audit experts, added, “We were actively looking for a cloud-based audit solution and, after reviewing several products, we selected Caseware for its functionality and integration. It has been a game-changer for us, particularly the risk assessment feature, which allows us to clearly identify key risks and design targeted responses. The software helps to customise the scope of each engagement in a smart and efficient way and, thanks to the integration with Cloud Financials, preparing statutory financial reports post-audit is now much faster and more streamlined.” 

Thanks to Caseware’s Artificial Intelligence Digital Assistant, Caseware AiDA™, customers will benefit from AI-powered tools within their audit workflow. Caseware AiDA provides real-time guidance for complex audit challenges and summarises detailed documents in seconds, turning complex information into actionable insights that drive better decision-making. Operating natively within Caseware Cloud, Caseware AiDA delivers consistent, secure integration into audit workflows for enhanced productivity. 

Key Features of Cloud Audit include: 

  • Real-Time Collaboration – audit teams can work simultaneously on engagements, with instant visibility into updates. 
  • Automatic Updates – ensures seamless deployment of compliance and template updates, reducing manual intervention. 
  • Built-in Analytics – provides real-time insights to support smarter, data-driven decision-making. 
  • Streamlined Reviews – enables managers and partners to easily review and oversee critical engagement areas. 
  • Enterprise-Grade Security – delivers advanced encryption and secure data hosting to protect sensitive client information. 

Cloud Audit also supports remote work, improves productivity through automation and allows firms to modernize audit processes in line with global digital trends. 

Showcasing innovation at ISCA Tech Fair 2025  
Caseware will participate in ISCA’s Tech Fair 2025, scheduled for July 11 at ISCA House. The event serves as a major platform for accounting professionals to explore emerging technologies and their applications within the profession. Located in the SMP-focused zone on Level 4, Caseware will host live product demonstrations and discussions focused on audit innovation. Attendees can engage with the Caseware team to explore how its solutions are used to enhance audit efficiency, collaboration and compliance across practice sizes. 

-ends- 

Attachment

  • Sarah Butler, Head of Solutions, APAC - Caseware 

Elise Sallis, VP, Head of Global Communications
Caseware
elise.sallis@caseware.com

data.title: South Korea Names Budget Expert as New Finance Minister
data.description:
By Kwanwoo Jun 




  South Korean President Lee Jae-myung has nominated Koo Yoon-cheol, a veteran bureaucrat and budget expert, as finance minister, as the government pushes for fiscal stimulus to support the sagging economy. 



  President Lee is still forming a new cabinet after taking office on June 4. The Lee administration will soon be seeking approval from the legislature for its additional budget proposal unveiled on June 19. 



  Koo, a former vice-finance minister, said at a hastily organized post-nomination press briefing in Seoul that he reaffirms Lee's expansionary fiscal policy and his artificial-intelligence investment push, according to the finance ministry. 



  Lee's extra budget package includes cash handouts to spur consumer spending and financial support for households and businesses vulnerable to the economic slowdown, as well as new investments in AI industries. 



  South Korea's economy is seen as losing steam. The Bank of Korea has recently downgraded its 2025 growth outlook, projecting the economy to grow 0.8% this year, less than half of 2024's 2.0% growth. 



  The export-led South Korean economy is also facing challenges from the tougher U.S. trade policy and higher tariffs under President Trump. 



  President Lee also nominated Kim Jung-kwan, a veteran bureaucrat-turned-head of nuclear-reactor and gas-turbine maker Doosan Enerbility, as minister of trade, industry and energy. 



  U.S. and South Korean trade negotiators reaffirmed their commitment to reach a tariff deal as early as possible, Seoul's minister for trade, Yeo Han-koo, said after meeting with U.S. Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick and U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer in Washington last week. 





  Write to Kwanwoo Jun at kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  June 29, 2025 21:07 ET (01:07 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: KKR to Sell Stake in Indian Pharma Company for $1.4 Billion
data.description:
By P.R. Venkat 




  Private equity firm KKR has agreed to sell its entire stake in India-listed J.B. Chemicals & Pharmaceuticals for $1.4 billion. 



  Under the deal, KKR will sell its 46.39% stake to Torrent Pharmaceuticals, the private-equity firm said late Sunday. The two pharmaceutical firms will then merge through a scheme of arrangement. 



  After J.B.'s merger with Torrent, each shareholder owning 100 shares of J.B. will receive 51 shares of Torrent. 



  "This strategic alignment furthers our goal of strengthening our presence in the Indian pharma market, and build a larger diversified global presence," Samir Mehta, Executive Chairman, Torrent said. 



  J.B., whose majority of revenue comes from the Indian market, also has a presence in Russia and South Africa. The company exports its finished formulations to over 40 countries, including the USA. It has eight manufacturing facilities in India, including a dedicated lozenge manufacturing facility. 



   Torrent Pharma concentrates on the chronic and sub-chronic therapeutic segments. It also has a global presence in countries like Brazil, Germany, and the U.S. 





  Write to P.R. Venkat at venkat.pr@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  June 29, 2025 20:39 ET (00:39 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: U.S. stock futures rise after S&P 500's latest record high
data.description:
By Mike Murphy

U.S. stock-market futures rose Sunday, after the S&P 500 and Nasdaq finished last week at record highs, indicating continued gains as the holiday-shortened week kicks off Monday.

Dow Jones Industrial Average futures (YM00) gained about 220 points, or 0.5%, late Sunday. S&P 500 futures (ES00) and Nasdaq-100 futures (NQ00) both rose about 0.3%. Crude prices (CL.1) dipped, along with gold (GC00). Bitcoin (BTCUSD) rallied, topping 108,000. The ICE U.S. Dollar Index DXY, a measure of the currency against a basket of six major rivals, slipped modestly.

All three major U.S. stock benchmarks booked strong weekly gains last week. The S&P 500 SPX rallied 3.4% to snap back-to-back weekly losses, notching its first record close since February. The tech-heavy Nasdaq COMP jumped 4.2% for the week, for its first record high since December. The Dow DJIA climbed 3.8%, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

Read more: S&P 500 scores record high for first time in 4 months. What could push stocks higher from here?

"Is it sustainable? That depends on the next landmine - Middle East, tariffs, Fed missteps - you name it," Stephen Innes, managing partner at SPI Asset Management, wrote in a note Sunday night. "However, price action suggests that the market is currently willing to believe in a larger rally. And for the first time this year, it's not just tech whispering that story - it's the whole room talking."

Also: Tech stocks are powering this record-setting rally on Wall Street - but how long can it last?

Stocks dipped briefly Friday after President Donald Trump said he would cut off trade discussions with Canada. On Sunday, Trump said in a Fox News interview that he's not planning on extending the pause on his global tariffs past the July 9 deadline. The White House has backed off its promise of 90 trade deals in 90 days, with Trump saying Sunday "you can't talk to all of them." Instead, the Trump administration plans to send letters to affected countries laying out trade penalties they will face.

"We'll look at how a country treats us - are they good, are they not so good - some countries we don't care, we'll just send a high number out," Trump said on Fox News's "Sunday Morning Futures." According to Trump, the letters will effectively say: "Congratulations, we're allowing you to shop in the United States of America, you're going to pay a 25% tariff, or 20% or 40 or 50%... That's the end of the trade deal."

More: A 2-month rally pushed the stock market to record highs - but watch for these risks in July

Meanwhile, Trump is hoping to get his "big, beautiful" tax and spending bill passed by the July 4 holiday. The legislation narrowly passed a procedural hurdle in the Senate on Saturday night, clearing the way for a vote in the coming days. But the Congressional Budget Office said Sunday the Republicans' tweaked bill will increase the federal deficit by neatly $3.3 trillion over the next decade, $1 trillion more than the House-approved bill. If approved by the full Senate, it would still need to get past a tightly divided House before heading to Trump's desk for final approval.

This week, investors will also be looking at employment data from three separate reports coming Tuesday, Wednesday and Thursday. Markets will be closed Friday for the July 4 holiday.

-Mike Murphy

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  06-29-25 2052ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Week Ahead for FX, Bonds: U.S. Jobs Data in Focus -2-
data.description:
Tuesday sees a flood of PMI prints across the region, including for Japan, India, Taiwan and South Korea. Markets will eye the surveys on manufacturing and services activity for insight into how tariff tensions are affecting business sentiment and growth projections.

S&P Global's June data showed a pullback in Asia's manufacturing activity, with U.S. tariffs continuing to cause steep declines in new orders.

U.S. trading partners are hesitant to commit to a trade deal, with numerous talks stalled as sectoral tariffs have yet to be announced for semiconductors, pharmaceuticals, and critical minerals, DBS Group Research's Chang Wei Liang said.

The U.S. seems to be moderating its trade policy stance given that the White House Press Secretary described its July deadline on the tariff pause as "not critical", which could signal a lesser risk of excessively high duties, Chang said. 




  Indonesia 





  Indonesia releases inflation on Tuesday that will likely back the case for policy easing. 



  Kenanga Investment Bank economist Muhammad Saifuddin Sapuan expects inflation to have stayed manageable in June, citing lower supply costs and declining energy prices. 



  This could give Bank Indonesia scope to resume interest-rate cuts, though the pace will depend on rupiah stability amid geopolitical risks, he added. 



  Trade data due the same day will show the impact of tariffs on Indonesia's exports. 



  Kenanga sees the surplus widening from April's surprising low, driven by frontloaded exports. 



  The U.S.-China tariff truce may offer Indonesia a temporary boost in trade sentiment by reducing regional uncertainty and supporting export demand. 





  Singapore 





  On Wednesday, eyes are on the manufacturing PMI data for June from the Singapore Institute of Purchasing and Materials Management. 



  The print will be watched for signs of deterioration in the manufacturing sector after the gauge contracted for a second consecutive month in May, signaling that global trade uncertainties are hurting the trade-reliant economy. 



  Singapore will also release retail sales figures for May on Friday. The data will give clues into how consumer demand is faring amid economic headwinds. 





  Philippines 





  The Philippines' inflation data for June will be released on Friday, shaping expectations for the central bank's next move. 



  The central bank cut rates again in June as inflation continued cooling, and it could do so again to bolster growth amid trade and geopolitical headwinds. 



  ING economists expect the figures to show that headline inflation edged up to 1.6% on the year in June from May's 1.3%, likely due to higher global oil prices. 



  "However, the increase in domestic fuel prices should be temporary and local pump prices are expected to fall in July," ING said. 





  Any references to days are in local times. 





  Write to Jessica Fleetham at jessica.fleetham@wsj.com and Fabiana Negrin Ochoa at fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  June 29, 2025 20:14 ET (00:14 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Week Ahead for FX, Bonds: U.S. Jobs Data in Focus as Fed Rate Cuts Loom
data.description:
By Dow Jones Newswires Staff 




  Below are the most important global events likely to affect FX and bond markets in the week starting June 30. 



  U.S. monthly jobs data will be the focus of the week as markets look to see whether the Federal Reserve could start cutting interest rates as soon as next month as political pressure for lower rates increases. 



  In Europe, focus will center on comments from central bankers around the globe at the European Central Bank's forum in Sintra, Portugal, as well as on flash eurozone inflation figures. In Asia, purchasing managers' surveys from China and the Bank of Japan's quarterly tankan corporate survey are scheduled. 





  U.S. 





  U.S. nonfarm payrolls figures for June will be closely watched because evidence of a weak job market could increase speculation that the Federal Reserve could cut interest rates soon, possibly as early as next month. 



  The data are released on Thursday this month because of the U.S. Independence Day holiday on Friday. 



  Fed Chair Jerome Powell has so far urged a cautious approach to cutting interest rates due to the risk that tariffs stoke inflation. However, a weak jobs report could be a trigger for an early move. 



  "The Fed would likely need to see clearer evidence of softness in the form of subdued payrolls growth and a rising unemployment rate to trigger an early move," ING chief international economist James Knightley said in a note. 



  President Trump has called for lower interest rates and the Wall Street Journal reported recently that he is considering naming his selection to replace Powell well ahead of Powell's term ending in 11 months' time. This sent the dollar to a three-year low against a basket of currencies. 



  Fed officials Christopher Waller and Michelle Bowman have also suggested they could opt for a rate reduction as soon as July. 



  Currently, U.S. money markets are pricing only a 20% chance of a rate reduction in July, instead fully pricing in a cut for September, LSEG data show. 



  "It won't take much for the market pricing for Fed easing to shift more convincingly to price a July cut," MUFG Bank analyst Derek Halpenny said in a note. 



  Ahead of Thursday's jobs report, investors will watch JOLTS job openings data for May on Tuesday and ADP private payrolls numbers for June on Wednesday for further clues on the health of the U.S. labor market. Weekly jobless claims are also released Thursday. 



  Other key indicators of how U.S. tariffs and uncertain politics are impacting economic activity will be Tuesday's ISM report on manufacturing activity in June and the equivalent reading for services activity on Thursday. U.S. trade data for May are due Thursday. 





  Canada 





  Canadian trade data for May are released Thursday and will be watched closely for an indication of the impact of U.S. tariffs. Data showed the Canadian economy contracted in both April and May as tariffs and trade threats weighed on activity. 





  Eurozone 





  The European Central Bank's annual forum in Sintra, Portugal, on central banking takes place between Monday and Wednesday. The theme of the forum is: "Adapting to change: macroeconomic shifts and policy responses." 



  It will feature speakers from the heads of the ECB, the Federal Reserve, the Bank of England and the Bank of Japan. 



  Provisional inflation data for the eurozone will mark the highlight of the week in terms of economic data. 



  Flash estimate inflation data from Germany and Italy are due Monday and from the eurozone on Tuesday. 



  Both eurozone headline and core HICP inflation are expected to have seen a 0.1% point fall, with the annual rates easing to 1.8% and 2.2% respectively, Investec's Ryan Djajasaputra said in a note. 



  "Factors behind this include a further moderation in services as well as in food price inflation, although we think this may be slightly offset by movements in energy and goods prices," he said. 



  German unemployment data is due on Tuesday, followed by similar Spanish, Italian and eurozone releases on Wednesday. The ECB's consumer expectations survey is also due on Tuesday. 



  Final French, German and eurozone manufacturing purchasing managers' index data for June, as well as figures from Spain and Italy, will be released on Tuesday. Services PMIs will follow on Thursday. 



  German manufacturing orders, French and Spanish industrial production for May and eurozone producer price inflation data are due Friday. 



  Germany will launch a new 10-year Bund on Wednesday with maturity in August 2035 and a volume of 6 billion euros. Spain and France will conduct auctions on Thursday. 





  U.K. 





  The second estimate for first-quarter U.K. gross domestic product will be released Monday. 



  The first estimate showed output expanded by a solid 0.7% compared with the previous quarter. The latest release will give additional details on household incomes and savings. 



  Any signs of weakness could increase prospects of another interest-rate cut from the Bank of England in August. Currently, U.K. money markets price in a 66% chance of an August reduction, LSEG data show. 



  Data on U.K. mortgage lending and approvals plus consumer credit for May are also scheduled for Monday. The final reading for the U.K. manufacturing purchasing managers' index for June is released Tuesday, followed by the equivalent services reading Thursday. 



  The U.K. plans to sell green gilts maturing in July 2053 on Tuesday, and March 2028 gilts on Wednesday. 





  Scandinavia 





  Swedish retail sales data for May are due on Monday. 



  Norway will conduct a bond auction on Wednesday. 





  Poland 





  Poland's central bank announces a decision on Wednesday, where a rate cut is possible. 



  Citi economists expect rates to be left on hold at 5.25% but acknowledge that this is "a close call." 



  "Our base case assumes that the MPC will choose to wait with rate cuts until September, as by that time it may receive more information on the direction of fiscal policy in 2026," Citi said in a note. 





  Japan 





  The Bank of Japan is scheduled to release its quarterly tankan corporate survey on Tuesday. 



  Policymakers are closely watching the data to see how much higher U.S. tariffs have affected sentiment and investment plans among Japanese businesses. The diffusion index measuring large manufacturers' mood is expected to deteriorate, according to a poll of economists by data provider Quick. 



  Auto sales figures for June also due Tuesday will likely give hints on the strength of domestic demand. 



  On Thursday, investors will look for any clues on the timing of the BOJ's next interest-rate increase in a speech by BOJ board member Hajime Takata. 



  The Japanese government is also scheduled to release industrial production data and household spending figures for May on Monday and Friday, respectively. 





  China 





  A string of PMIs shed light on manufacturing and services activity in China at the end of the second quarter. 



  Markets will hone in on export orders trends, as tariffs keep hurting demand for Chinese goods. 



  China's official manufacturing PMI signaled some improvement in May, but remained in contraction territory, pointing to ongoing challenges in the sector. The private Caixin gauge, meanwhile, tumbled into contraction, sending a worrying sign about the economy. 



  ING economists look for the official manufacturing PMI for June to remain in contraction but show a modest uptick to 49.8. Markets are looking for the official non-manufacturing PMI to remain broadly unchanged, they said. 



  The two official gauges are due Monday, followed by the Caixin Manufacturing PMI Tuesday and the services PMI on Thursday. 



  Traders will also stay on alert for any comments from Chinese officials on trade discussions with the U.S., amid signs of easing tensions over rare-earth exports - a key point of friction in bilateral talks. 





  Australia 





  Australian bond traders will focus on May consumer spending data, which will be the last major indicator before the Reserve Bank of Australia's next policy meeting that starts July 7. 



  Still, it will take evidence of a sharp recovery in consumer spending from the retail sales data on Wednesday and the household spending indicator on Friday to derail what are confident bets among economists that the RBA will deliver its third interest rate cut this easing cycle in July. 



  Recent inflation data has shown that price pressures have diminished more quickly than expected, opening a path for the RBA to cut again next month, and indicate that more cuts are likely before the end of the year. 



  RBA Gov. Michele Bullock may also take the opportunity to review the bank's overarching narrative, moving it away from the fight against inflation to a more pro-growth outlook. 



  The economy has been misfiring over recent quarters, and the RBA could be doing more to foster a recovery in private-sector business activity. 





  South Korea 





  South Korea is due to release its June trade data on Tuesday. Exports likely rebounded largely on brisk demand for semiconductors amid the global artificial-intelligence boom. 



  Outbound shipments are expected to have risen 4.9% from a year earlier, following a 1.3% fall in May, according to a poll by The Wall Street Journal. Imports likely increased 8.0% on year, resulting in a $7.47 billion surplus for June. 



  Resilient tech-driven exports despite higher U.S. tariffs could prompt the Bank of Korea to cut interest rates at a slower pace, Citigroup economist Jin-Wook Kim said. 



  Inflation data for June due Wednesday is likely to show that headline inflation picked up, and kept hovering around the central bank's 2% annual target. A WSJ poll sees a rise of 2.1% on the year, following a 1.9% gain in May. The de-escalation of Middle East tensions may limit the impact of oil prices on inflation, iM Securities chief economist Park Sang-hyun said. 





  Asia PMIs 





  (MORE TO FOLLOW) Dow Jones Newswires



  June 29, 2025 20:14 ET (00:14 GMT)

data.title: U.S. stock futures rise after S&P 500's latest record high
data.description:
By Mike Murphy

U.S. stock-market futures rose Sunday, after the S&P 500 and Nasdaq finished last week at record highs, indicating continued gains as the holiday-shortened week kicks off Monday.

Dow Jones Industrial Average futures (YM00) gained about 210 points, or 0.5%, late Sunday. S&P 500 futures (ES00) and Nasdaq-100 futures (NQ00) both rose about 0.3%. Crude prices (CL.1) dipped, along with gold (GC00). Bitcoin (BTCUSD) rallied, topping 108,000. The ICE U.S. Dollar Index DXY, a measure of the currency against a basket of six major rivals, slipped modestly.

All three major U.S. stock benchmarks booked strong weekly gains last week. The S&P 500 SPX rallied 3.4% to snap back-to-back weekly losses, notching its first record close since February. The tech-heavy Nasdaq COMP jumped 4.2% for the week, for its first record high since December. The Dow DJIA climbed 3.8%, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

Read more: S&P 500 scores record high for first time in 4 months. What could push stocks higher from here?

Stocks dipped briefly Friday after President Donald Trump said he would cut off trade discussions with Canada. On Sunday, Trump said in a Fox News interview that he's not planning on extending the pause on his global tariffs past the July 9 deadline. The White House has backed off its promise of 90 trade deals in 90 days, with Trump saying Sunday "you can't talk to all of them." Instead, the Trump administration plans to send letters to affected countries laying out trade penalties they will face.

"We'll look at how a country treats us - are they good, are they not so good - some countries we don't care, we'll just send a high number out," Trump said on Fox News's "Sunday Morning Futures." According to Trump, the letters will effectively say: "Congratulations, we're allowing you to shop in the United States of America, you're going to pay a 25% tariff, or 20% or 40 or 50%... That's the end of the trade deal."

More: A 2-month rally pushed the stock market to record highs - but watch for these risks in July

Meanwhile, Trump is hoping to get his "big, beautiful" tax and spending bill passed by the July 4 holiday. The legislation narrowly passed a procedural hurdle in the Senate on Saturday night, clearing the way for a vote in the coming days. But the Congressional Budget Office said Sunday the Republicans' tweaked bill will increase the federal deficit by neatly $3.3 trillion over the next decade, $1 trillion more than the House-approved bill. If approved by the full Senate, it would still need to get past a tightly divided House before heading to Trump's desk for final approval.

This week, investors will also be looking at employment data from three separate reports coming Tuesday, Wednesday and Thursday. Markets will be closed Friday for the July 4 holiday.

-Mike Murphy

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  06-29-25 2005ET
